title: Paper clay sculpted owl
subtitle: A standing owl figure approximately 120 mm tall
excerpt: A hand-built paper clay owl standing approximately 120 mm tall, formed from a pinched body with slab wings, modelled face, and coil feet. No kiln needed. PVA sealed and left natural white or painted with acrylics.

glossaryTerms.definition: air-dry clay with cellulose fibres added, which bridge cracks and strengthen thin sections
the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
clay diluted with water to a brushable or pourable consistency
a joining technique where both surfaces are scratched (scored) and coated with wet clay (slip) before pressing together to create a secure bond

content.text: This standing owl is approximately 120 mm tall from base to ear tufts, built from
paper clay

using a pinched body, rolled slab wings, a modelled face, and coil feet. The cellulose fibres in paper clay allow thin joints between the wings and body to dry without cracking, which makes it better suited to this project than plain air-dry clay. No kiln is required. The finished piece is sealed with PVA and can be left the natural white of the clay or painted with acrylic colours.
Preparation
Mix a small cup of
slip
from paper clay scraps dissolved in water. It should be the consistency of thick cream. This is your jointing medium for
score-and-slip

joins. Paper clay can also be joined wet-to-wet without scoring at very early stages, but scoring gives the most reliable result for pieces that will be displayed or handled. Have a small bowl of water and the damp pottery sponge nearby. Build the body first so it has time to firm slightly while you prepare the other parts.
Construction

Body: take 150 g of paper clay and roll it into a smooth ball. Begin pinching from the top, rotating the ball steadily between your hands, pressing the walls outward and upward to form a thick oval. The body should be about 80 mm tall and 60 mm wide with walls approximately 10 mm thick. A thicker base is deliberate: it provides stability for the standing owl. Smooth the outside with a damp sponge.

Tail: pinch a small wedge of clay, about 20 mm wide and 15 mm long, and flatten it into a flat fan shape 5 mm thick. Use score-and-slip to attach it to the rear base of the body, angled slightly downward. The tail extends below the body and forms a third contact point with the surface, giving the owl stability without a separate stand.

Wings: roll a slab 5 mm thick using the rolling pin. Cut two wing shapes with the fettling knife: each wing is roughly teardrop-shaped, 70 mm long and 30 mm at its widest. Round the lower tip. Score the inner face of each wing and the corresponding area on each side of the body. Apply slip to both surfaces and press the wings firmly against the sides of the body, angled slightly forward and downward. Hold in place for 30 seconds to allow the slip to begin bonding. Smooth the join line with a wet fingertip.

Feet: roll two coil logs of clay, each 60 mm long and 8 mm in diameter. Flatten each coil slightly. Score the top of each coil and the base of the body in two corresponding positions. Apply slip and press on, splaying each foot slightly to the side for a stable three-point base (two feet and the tail). Blend the join into the body base with a wet fingertip.

Eye discs: pinch two small clay balls approximately 15 mm in diameter and flatten each to a disc 5 mm thick. Score the backs and the corresponding positions on the face. Apply slip and press in place, side by side in the upper third of the body. Press a small indentation into the centre of each disc with the tip of a pencil or the blunt end of the needle tool.

Beak: roll a small ball of clay roughly 8 mm in diameter and pinch it into a short triangular form. Score both surfaces and apply slip. Press the beak between and below the two eye discs. Blend the edges into the face with a damp fingertip.

Ear tufts: pinch two small elongated points of clay, each about 15 mm tall and 8 mm wide at the base. Score and slip them onto the top of the head, one on each side of the centre line, angled slightly outward. These are thin elements where the paper clay's cellulose fibre is most beneficial in preventing cracking.

Once all parts are joined, use the needle tool to add feather texture across the body and wings: short, curved lines drawn from the wing tips inward and upward, following the direction feathers grow on a bird. Keep the lines light so they read as surface texture rather than deep cuts.

Stand the owl upright on the wooden board. Check it stands without leaning: adjust the feet and tail placement while all joins are still fresh if necessary. The owl should rest on two feet and the tail tip.
Drying

Leave the owl standing on the wooden board at room temperature for 48 to 3 days. Paper clay dries more slowly at joins where two layers of clay meet. Do not attempt to move the figure to a different surface until it has passed the
leather-hard
stage. At leather-hard the joins are stable enough to handle carefully; continue drying until completely
bone-dry
. If any ear tufts or wing tips develop hairline cracks during drying, apply a small amount of paper clay slip to the crack with the mop brush and allow to dry again before sealing.
Finishing

Apply undiluted PVA to all surfaces with the mop brush. Work into the feather-texture grooves. Allow to dry, then apply a second coat. Once fully sealed, the owl can be left its natural white or painted with acrylic colours: warm tawny brown over the back and wings, cream on the face disc and belly, and dark brown or black pupils in the eye centres. Apply the PVA seal after painting rather than before if painting with acrylics.
Care

Display on a flat indoor surface away from moisture. The ear tufts and wing tips are the most fragile points. Dust with a dry soft brush rather than a cloth to avoid snagging the thin elements. If a joined part separates during use, re-adhere with PVA or a small amount of fresh paper clay slip pressed into the join, dried flat, and resealed.
Variations
Make a family grouping: one large owl at 120 mm and two smaller owls at 60 mm, all in the same construction sequence at reduced scale.
Add a branch: roll a thick clay coil into a curved branch shape and attach to the feet instead of standing the owl on a flat base.
Pierce a small hole through the top of the head for a cord before drying, and hang the owl as a wall or window ornament.
Use the same construction method to build other bird forms: a round robin or a squat puffin both suit the pinched-body approach.

rows.problem: Wing separates from body during drying
rows.cause: Score-and-slip join not pressed firmly enough, or clay too dry at time of joining
rows.fix: Re-attach using fresh paper clay slip on both surfaces, press and hold for one minute, then smooth the join edge. If the clay was too dry, wet the join area with water first, wait five minutes, then score and reapply.

rows.problem: Ear tufts crack and break off
rows.cause: Thin clay elements dry faster than the thick body and crack under differential shrinkage
rows.fix: Paper clay slip repairs these well: apply slip to the break, hold the tuft in place and support it with a small rolled paper prop until dry. The cellulose fibres bridge the crack.

rows.cause: Wings heavier than body supports, or feet not angled to counterbalance
rows.fix: Prop the body upright with a small rolled-up cloth or paper wad on each side while it dries at leather-hard stage. Adjust foot angle before bone-dry.

rows.cause: Lines too shallow or clay too wet when textured
rows.fix: Apply texture at leather-hard, not while the clay is freshly built. Shallow lines deepen slightly as they dry but very faint lines vanish. Re-incise at leather-hard with firmer pressure if the first pass was too light.

rows.problem: PVA seals over crack lines and makes them more visible
rows.cause: Hairline cracks sealed without filling first
rows.fix: Fill cracks with paper clay slip and let dry completely before applying PVA seal. The PVA over an unfilled crack darkens the line.
